output.final_deliverable:
Missing required evidence: the merged results include only search result snippets and do not include any visited-page content (no extracted text, no primary-document passages, and no verified archival records). The mandate requires visiting URLs and quoting at least one passage from a historical document or contemporary news article; that quotation cannot be produced from the merged results alone.

What would need to be visited (examples from merged search results):
- Contemporary/archival newspaper reprints (SMH “From the Archives, 1932: First shots fired in the 'Emu War'”; WAtoday “From the Archives, 1932: The Great Emu War begins”) to extract a direct quotation and confirm publication context: https://www.smh.com.au/national/from-the-archives-1932-first-shots-fired-in-the-emu-war-20221025-p5bsux.html ; https://www.watoday.com.au/national/western-australia/from-the-archives-1932-the-great-emu-war-begins-20221027-p5bth1.html
- Parliamentary record (Hansard) referenced in secondary reporting (e.g., Guardian article referencing questions put to Defence Minister Pearce): https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2023/oct/06/australias-emu-war-john-cleese-outrun-in-race-to-shoot-movie-of-how-flightless-birds-thwarted-armys-machine-guns
- Government archival catalogue entries (National Archives of Australia) for the actual file(s) containing Meredith’s report / correspondence (a search result pointing to NAA isn’t present as a URL in the merged results; a specific NAA item link is missing and would be required to cite it as a historical record).
- Modern analysis sources for synthesis (to be visited for concrete claims and citations): Encyclopaedia Britannica https://www.britannica.com/topic/Emu-War ; Australian Geographic https://www.australiangeographic.com.au/nature-wildlife/2016/10/australias-emu-wars/ ; DOAJ-hosted article “The Emu Strikes Back: An Inquiry Into Australia’s Peculiar Military Action of 1932” https://doaj.org/article/b7ed3a4b7bb74d5685e22088c69a736b ; National Geographic overview (may be paywalled) https://www.nationalgeographic.com/history/article/australian-emu-war-history

Because no visits/extractions are included in the merged results, I cannot (from this evidence) provide: (1) a verified primary-source quotation, (2) specific archival file identifiers/links for government records, or (3) properly evidenced modern analysis claims beyond what is hinted in snippets. Please provide visited-page extracts or allow a new run that performs search→visit and captures quotations and bibliographic details.
output.action_summary: The merged input contains only search-result snippets and URLs; it lacks visited-page evidence. Therefore the request’s core requirements—primary documents/historical records, modern analysis grounded in visited sources, and at least one direct quotation from a historical document or contemporary news article—cannot be completed from the provided evidence. Key URLs to visit next are SMH and WAtoday archival reprints, Hansard (as referenced by the Guardian), and modern analyses like Britannica, Australian Geographic, and the DOAJ journal article.

5e9c8d4d-cff8-4957-acde-6921e058986a.title:
Research the 'Great Emu War' of Australia. Find primary source documents or historical records, modern analysis of the event, and quote at least one passage from a historical document or contemporary news article about it. Provide URLs to all sources.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

details.mandate:
Research the 'Great Emu War' of Australia. Find primary source documents or historical records, modern analysis of the event, and quote at least one passage from a historical document or contemporary news article about it. Provide URLs to all sources.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

merged_results.title: Collect primary sources/contemporary reporting on the 1932 emu campaign (parliamentary records + 1932 newspapers) and extract at least one quotable passage

result.query: 1932 emu war Western Australia parliamentary debate Hansard emus machine guns Meredith report quote
result.intent: Find primary-source documents (Hansard/parliament papers or 1932 newspapers) that contain directly quotable passages about the emu campaign; identify URLs to visit for full text extraction.

results.title: Australia’s emu war: John Cleese outrun in race to shoot movie of how flightless birds thwarted army’s machine guns | Western Australia | The Guardian
results.description: Pearce was forced to defend himself against questions about whether it was “possible to kill the emus by more humane, if less spectacular, methods” than machine guns, according to Hansard.

results.title: The bizarre story of when Australia went to war with emus—and lost | National Geographic
results.description: The lead officer, Gwynydd Purves Wynne-Aubrey Meredith, famously said, “[Emus] could face machine guns with the invulnerability of tanks.” · Forty-five days after the start of the campaign, the soldiers had killed only about 2,500 emus—a ...

results.title: Emu War - Wikipedia
results.description: Military involvement was due to begin in October 1932. The "war" was conducted under the command of Major Gwynydd Purves Wynne-Aubrey Meredith of the Royal Australian Artillery's 7th Heavy Artillery, with Meredith commanding soldiers Sergeant S. McMurray and Gunner J. O'Halloran, armed with two Lewis guns and 10,000 rounds of ammunition. The operation was delayed by a period of rainfall that caused the emus ...

results.title: Emu War | Australia, Casualties, History, Summary, & Facts | Britannica
results.description: Emu War, short-lived and ultimately unsuccessful military operation to address the issue of emus—large flightless birds native to Australia—damaging large amounts of crops in Western Australia. The campaign lasted from November to December 1932. Three members of the Royal Australian Artillery were assigned to cull roughly 20,000 emus using machine guns, but both the weapons and tactics employed proved wholly unsuited for the task.

results.title: The Great Emu War - by Ben A. Wajdi - Wajdi Archives
results.description: Australian House of Representatives, Hansard, November 8, 1932. ... UK newspaper coverage compiled in "Emus vs.

results.title: Emu War facts for kids
results.description: He said, "If we had a military division with the bullet-carrying capacity of these birds it would face any army in the world... They can face machine guns with the invulnerability of tanks.

results.title: The Great Emu War of 1932: Australia’s Feathered Foe
results.description: The Australian House of Representatives debated the issue, and after considerable embarrassment, the military was withdrawn from the operation. The farmers were left to deal with the emus on their own, and the government eventually resorted to offering bounties on the birds, which proved to be a more effective method of control. Major Meredith, despite the failure of the campaign, reportedly had a sense of humor about the whole affair.

results.title: The Great Emu War – Retrospect Journal
results.description: Meredith wrote about the emus saying; “If we had a military division with the bullet-carrying capacity of these birds it would face any army in the world…They can face machine guns with the invulnerability of tanks.

merged_results.title: Find and verify official archival/government records about the operation (National Archives of Australia, WA State Records, Defence/PM&C files)

result.query: National Archives of Australia emu war 1932 file Meredith report emus Campion Walgoolan
result.intent: Locate archival catalog entries or digitized government documents about the 1932 operation to use as historical records/primary sources; gather URLs for follow-up visits.

results.title: From the Archives, 1932: First shots fired in the 'Emu War'
results.description: A boundary rider on the rabbit-proof fence reports that three large mobs of emus are heading for the Campion district from the dry northern areas in search of feed and water. Major Meredith stated that it was possible that the gun crews and ...

results.title: From the Archives, 1932: The Great Emu War begins
results.description: Mr Lambert said the interruption of the campaign was made all the more unexpected and irritating by the fact that on Saturday he telegraphed Major Meredith, who has charge of the Campion party, asking him if the infestation in that district would justify an extension of the operations to Walggolan. Major Meredith had replied that they would. The farmers were perturbed at the decision of the Minister of Defence. He has received a telegram from the representative stating that it was imperative that the gunners should stay as emus were beginning to appear in large numbers.

results.title: Emu War - Wikipedia
results.description: Although the military had agreed to lend the guns to the Western Australian government on the expectation that they would provide the necessary people, Meredith was once again placed in the field due to an apparent lack of experienced machine gunners in the state. Taking to the field on 13 November 1932, the military found a degree of success over the first two days, with approximately 40 emus killed.

results.title: The Great Emu War: How Flightless Birds Beat the Australian Army | History Hit
results.description: On 13 November, Meredith returned following requests by farmers and reports that more birds had been killed than had earlier been suggested. Over the next month, the soldiers slayed around 100 emus every week.

results.title: Looking back: Australia's Emu Wars - Australian Geographic
results.description: Within a week of first contact the troops were recalled. Roughly 2500 rounds had been fired and killed somewhere between 50 and 200 emus. Meredith did note that his men had suffered no casualties.

results.title: Emu War | Australia, Casualties, History, Summary, & Facts | Britannica
results.description: While farmers were permitted to shoot the emus themselves, their resources were limited, leading them to request government assistance. Minister for Defence George Pearce believed that dispatching professional soldiers to help the farmers would demonstrate that the government took veterans’ concerns seriously. In November 1932 three members of the Royal Australian Artillery traveled to the Campion district: Maj. Gwynydd Purves Wynne-Aubrey Meredith in command, Sgt.

results.title: Emu War of 1932 - Stuff You Missed in History Class | iHeart
results.description: It was not as the scessful attempt well, and it most likely actually made things worse, since the fleeing EMUs than not only had they been eating week, but now they were trampling crops. So the (15:10): soldier settlers, though, continued to ask for aid, and Meredith's reports spoke of the extensive damage that the emu's had done.

results.title: The Great Emu War – Retrospect Journal
results.description: ‘Use of Machine Guns for Eradication of Emus in WA and Wild Horses in North Queensland – [Page 40, 53 and 54 – Emu Eradication – Campion District dated 10 November 1932]’ (1932).

merged_results.title: Gather modern scholarly or institutional analysis (museum/university/encyclopedia-quality writeups) with clear citations/backreferences to primary sources

result.query: analysis of the Great Emu War 1932 Western Australia scholarly article museum university
result.intent: Identify modern secondary analyses that summarize the event with references to primary sources; collect URLs to visit and extract key interpretations and context.

results.title: (PDF) The Emu Strikes Back: An Inquiry Into Australia's Peculiar Military Action of 1932
results.description: The Great Emu War of 1932 exemplifies a bizarre military failure in Australian history. The Australian Army used Lewis machine guns and fired 9,860 rounds, killing only 986 emus. Major G.P.W. Meredith led a poorly planned operation against 20,000 ...

results.title: Emu War - Wikipedia
results.description: Pearce also supported the deployment on the grounds that the birds would make good target practice, while it has also been argued that some in the government may have viewed the operation as a way of being seen to be helping the Western Australian farmers, as well as to stave off the nascent secession movement. Toward that end, a cinematographer from Fox Movietone was enlisted. Military involvement was due to begin in October 1932.

results.title: Rise of the Emu-pire: The Emu War, 1932 — NAIMUN LXIII
results.description: Following World War I, the Australian ... in Western Australia, encouraging them to take up farming in the country’s interior. By the early 1930s, however, these farmers were facing harsh economic conditions brought on by the Great Depression and plummeting wheat prices. To make matters worse, they found themselves contending with a new threat – tens of thousands of emus migrating inland from the coast during their annual breeding season. In late 1932, the emus ...

results.title: Emu War | Australia, Casualties, History, Summary, & Facts | Britannica
results.description: Emu War, short-lived and ultimately unsuccessful military operation to address the issue of emus—large flightless birds native to Australia—damaging large amounts of crops in Western Australia.

results.title: The Great Emu War – Retrospect Journal
results.description: Elizabeth Hall details the 'Great Emu War' of Australia, considering the worldwide economic instability which caused it, as well as how farmers and the Australian Government reacted.

results.title: (PDF) The Emu Strikes Back: An Inquiry into Australia's Peculiar Military Action of 1932
results.description: Military failures such as the infamous Great Emu War of 1932 Western Australia are arguably difficult to match in military history. In this article, we tackle the Seventh Heavy Battery of the Royal Australian Artillery’s attempt to ‘wage ...

results.title: THE EMU STRIKES BACK: AN INQUIRY INTO AUSTRALIA’S PECULIAR MILITARY ACTION OF 1932 – DOAJ
results.description: Military failures such as the infamous Great Emu War of 1932 Western Australia are arguably difficult to match in military history. In this article, we tackle the Seventh Heavy Battery of the Royal Australian Artillery’s attempt to ‘wage ...

results.title: The bizarre story of when Australia went to war with emus—and lost | National Geographic
results.description: However, the region’s harsh ... rainfall. When a severe drought in 1932 drove nearly 20,000 emus into the farming areas, the birds—breaking fences that allowed smaller pests in—became the final straw....
